name: fever spirits
shortDescription: The fever spirits were minor deities in Mesopotamian mythology, believed to be agents of plague and disease, often associated with Nergal, the god of the underworld.
description: The fever spirits, also known as the Asakku, played a significant role in Mesopotamian beliefs about disease and misfortune. They were considered demons or minor deities that brought illness and fever upon humans, acting as agents of divine punishment or simply as forces of chaos. Often associated with Nergal, the god of the underworld, plague, and war, they were seen as his offspring or servants, carrying out his will on earth. Mesopotamians believed that these spirits could enter the body and cause sickness. Consequently, a significant part of Mesopotamian medicine focused on exorcising these malevolent entities through incantations, rituals, and the use of amulets. The belief in fever spirits highlights the Mesopotamian understanding of disease as a supernatural phenomenon, rather than a purely natural one. Their existence was interwoven with the larger pantheon, especially concerning death, the underworld, and the power of the gods to influence human life. While not as prominent as the major deities, the fever spirits were a constant presence in the daily lives of Mesopotamians, who sought protection and healing from their perceived attacks.

sources.title: Myths from Mesopotamia: Creation, The Flood, Gilgamesh, and Others
sources.author: Stephanie Dalley
